questions.text: When a particle moves in a vertical circle:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: It has constant radial and tangential acceleration
options.explanation: When the particle is moving in a vertical circle, the force towards the centre and along the tangent vary from point to point and so its radial and tangential accelerations vary.

options.text: It has variable tangential and radial accelerations
options.explanation: In a vertical circle, such as when an object is swinging on a string or a roller coaster is moving along a loop, the motion is not characterized by constant acceleration. The direction of motion and the forces acting on the object change throughout the motion. As a result, both tangential (in the direction of motion) and radial (inward or outward) accelerations vary as the object moves through the different points of the circle. At the top of the circle, for instance, the tangential acceleration may be directed downward, while at the bottom, it may be directed upward, resulting in varying acceleration values. Similarly, the radial acceleration changes as the object moves around the circle. This makes option (b) the correct choice.

options.text: It only has constant radial acceleration
options.explanation: Since particle is moving in circle, its radial acceleration varies from point to point.

options.text: It only has constant tangential acceleration
options.explanation: Since particle is moving in a circle, its direction changes from point to point , therefore tangential acceleration changes.

questions.text: A disc revolves in a horizontal plane at a steady rate of 3 rad/s. A coin, when placed on the disc at 20 cm from the axis of rotation, just starts to slip. The coefficient of friction (g = π2 m/s2) is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Incorrect as per the formula
Incorrect as per the formula

This is the following solution: Ff= μ Fn Since the coin is just about to slip, the maximum value of static friction (Ff-max) is acting on the coin, and it is equal to: Ff-max = μ Fn-max Now, the normal force (Fn-max) can be calculated as the gravitational force acting on the coin: Fn-max = mg where: g is the acceleration due to gravity (given as π2 m/s2). Now, substituting the expressions for Fc and Fn_max into the equation for static friction: μ mg = m ω2 r Solving for the coefficient of friction (μ): μ = (m ω2 r) / (m g) m and m (mass of the coin) cancel out from both sides:μ = (ω2 r) / g Now, we can plug in the given values: ω = 3 rad/s r = 0.2 meters g = π2 m/s2 μ = (32 x 0.2) / π2 μ ≈ 0.18 Therefore, the coefficient of friction (μ) between the coin and the revolving disc is approximately 0.18.

questions.text: In the given diagram, with what force must the man pull the rope to hold the plank in position? The weight of the man is 60 x 9.81 N. Neglect the weights of the plank, rope, and pulley.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is the following solution: We are given a system in which a man is standing on a plank which is attached to two pulleys through ropes as shown in the figure. The man is pulling the end of a rope that is attached to the pulley 2 so that the whole system will be at rest. We can understand that since the weights of the pulleys, ropes, and the plank are to be neglected, the only force that needs to be balanced is the weight due to the man’s mass on the plank in the downward direction. i.e., F total=0 Let us consider the man on the plank and the tension on the rope on pulley 2 due to the force he exerts as – T+N=mg ∴T+N−mg=0 --(1) Now, let us consider the normal reaction of the plank and the tensions on the ropes connected to pulleys 1 and 2 as – 2T+T=N ∴3T−N=0 --(2) Let us substitute (1) in (2) to the value for the normal reaction as – 3T−N=0 ⇒3(mg−N)−N=0 ∴N=3/4mg Now, we can substitute the above relation in (1) to get the tension on the rope which will give the necessary force as – T+N−mg=0 ⇒T+(3/4mg−mg)=0 ⇒T=1/4mg ⇒T=1/4(60)kg×g ∴T=15kg×g= 15×9.81 N This is the required force to be exerted by the man to keep the plank in position. The correct answer is option A.

questions.text: A mass of 1 kg is suspended by a thread. It is: (i) lifted up with an acceleration of 4.9 ms–2 (ii) lowered down with an acceleration of 4.9 ms–2 The ratio of tensions in the thread is:

Pseudo force on a body = The mass of the body × The accelerating of the observer(in opposite direction) Acceleration due to gravity(g) = 10 ms-2 The pseudo force on the block of mass 2kg for an observer in the elevator is given as; = Mass of the block × Acceleration of the observer in the elevator = 2×g/2 The pseudo force on the block of mass 4kg for an observer in the elevator is given as: =4×g/2 If the acceleration in the blocks is a, then For the block of mass of 4kg; (4kg×g+4kg×g/2)−T=4kg×a 4g+4g/2−T=4a..............(1) For the block of mass 2kg; T−2kg×g/2−2kg×g=2kg×a T−2g/2−2g=2a 2T−2g−4g=4a 2T−6g=4a................(2) From equations (1) and (2), we get 4g+4g/2−T=2T−6g ⇒2T−6g=4g+4g/2−T ⇒4T−12g=8g+4g−2T ⇒6T=24g ⇒T=4g ⇒T=4×10ms−2 ⇒T=40N

questions.text: A gun after firing recoils due to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Conservation of energy states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transferred or transformed. In the case of a gun firing, conservation of energy is not the primary reason for the recoil. While energy is involved in the process, it does not directly explain the recoil of the gun.

A gun after firing recoils due to conversation of momentum. Conservation of momentum states that the total momentum of a closed system remains constant unless acted upon by external forces. When a gun is fired, the bullet gains forward momentum. According to the conservation of momentum, an equal and opposite momentum must be imparted to the gun. This is the reason for the recoil experienced by the gun after firing.

When a gun is fired, the expanding gases produced by the combustion of gunpowder push the bullet forward, propelling it out of the barrel. However, according to Newton's third law of motion, the action-reaction forces act on different objects. The force exerted by the expanding gases acts on the bullet, not on the gun itself. As a result, the backward thrust of gases alone does not directly cause the recoil of the gun.

Newton's first law, also known as the law of inertia, states that an object at rest will remain at rest, and an object in motion will continue moving at a constant velocity unless acted upon by external forces. While Newton's first law is relevant to the recoil of the gun, it doesn't fully explain it. The recoil is primarily due to the conservation of momentum.

questions.text: A shell explodes into four unequal parts. Which one of the following is conserved?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Potential energy is the energy stored in an object due to its position or configuration. In the case of a shell explosion, the potential energy is converted into kinetic energy as the parts move away from each other. However, potential energy is not conserved because it is converted into other forms of energy (kinetic energy, sound energy, etc.) during the explosion.

Kinetic energy is the energy an object possesses due to its motion. In a shell explosion, the initial potential energy is converted into kinetic energy as the parts move apart. The kinetic energy of the individual parts may not be equal due to their unequal masses and velocities. Thus, kinetic energy is not conserved in this scenario.

Momentum is the product of an object's mass and its velocity. It is a vector quantity, meaning it has both magnitude and direction. Momentum is conserved in an isolated system, which means that the total momentum of the system remains constant before and after the explosion. In the case of a shell exploding into four unequal parts, the total momentum of the system (the sum of momenta of all the parts) remains the same before and after the explosion, provided no external forces act on the system during the explosion. This is because momentum is transferred between the parts as they move away from each other, leading to the conservation of the total momentum.

As discussed earlier, potential energy and kinetic energy are not individually conserved during the explosion of the shell. While energy is conserved overall (by the law of conservation of energy), it is not conserved in each form separately. Therefore, option d) is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Option c) Momentum is the correct option that is conserved when a shell explodes into four unequal parts. Let's explain each option: a) PE (Potential Energy): Potential energy is the energy stored in an object due to its position or configuration. In the case of a shell explosion, the potential energy is converted into kinetic energy as the parts move away from each other. However, potential energy is not conserved because it is converted into other forms of energy (kinetic energy, sound energy, etc.) during the explosion. b) KE (Kinetic Energy): Kinetic energy is the energy an object possesses due to its motion. In a shell explosion, the initial potential energy is converted into kinetic energy as the parts move apart. The kinetic energy of the individual parts may not be equal due to their unequal masses and velocities. Thus, kinetic energy is not conserved in this scenario. c) Momentum: Momentum is the product of an object's mass and its velocity. It is a vector quantity, meaning it has both magnitude and direction. Momentum is conserved in an isolated system, which means that the total momentum of the system remains constant before and after the explosion. In the case of a shell exploding into four unequal parts, the total momentum of the system (the sum of momenta of all the parts) remains the same before and after the explosion, provided no external forces act on the system during the explosion. This is because momentum is transferred between the parts as they move away from each other, leading to the conservation of the total momentum. d) Both PE and KE: As discussed earlier, potential energy and kinetic energy are not individually conserved during the explosion of the shell. While energy is conserved overall (by the law of conservation of energy), it is not conserved in each form separately. Therefore, option d) is incorrect. In conclusion, the correct option is c) Momentum because it is the quantity that remains conserved when a shell explodes into four unequal parts.

options.explanation: Range refers to the horizontal distance covered by a projectile during its motion. In other words, it is the distance from the point of projection to the point where the projectile hits the ground again. The range depends on the initial velocity of the projectile, the launch angle, and the acceleration due to gravity. It is an important quantity to consider, especially in applications like ballistics, sports, and engineering.

Trajectory is the path followed by a projectile through space. It is a three-dimensional curve that describes the motion of the projectile as it moves under the influence of gravity and any other forces acting on it. The trajectory considers both the horizontal and vertical components of the projectile's motion. It can be a parabolic curve for a projectile launched in a uniform gravitational field with no air resistance.
"Cycle" does not relate to the path followed by a projectile. In physics, a cycle typically refers to a complete round trip or a repetitive pattern, such as in oscillatory motion.

It is not specific to the path followed by a projectile but can be used to describe the maximum height reached by a projectile during its flight. The maximum height occurs when the vertical component of the projectile's velocity becomes zero.

questions.text: The P.E gained by projectile when it reaches at maximum height is expressed by equation;

The potential energy (PE) of a projectile at its maximum height is equal to its kinetic energy (KE) at the point of projection. This is because the projectile has no vertical velocity at its maximum height, so all of its energy is in the form of potential energy. The equation for PE is: PE = mgh where: m is the mass of the projectile g is the acceleration due to gravity h is the height of the projectile The equation for KE is: KE = 1/2mv² where: m is the mass of the projectile v is the velocity of the projectile At the point of projection, the velocity of the projectile is v = vo, where vo is the initial velocity of the projectile. Substituting these equations into the equation for PE, we get: PE = mgh = 1/2mvo² Solving for h, we get: h = 1/2vo²/g The maximum height of the projectile is reached when the vertical component of its velocity is zero. This happens when the projectile has reached its maximum height and is momentarily at rest. The vertical component of the velocity of the projectile is given by: v = vo sin θ where: θ is the angle of projection Setting v = 0 and solving for θ, we get: θ = sin⁻¹(0/vo) The angle of projection at which the projectile reaches its maximum height is always 90 degrees. Therefore, the PE gained by the projectile when it reaches its maximum height is: PE = 1/2vo²/g This is equal to K.E. sin²θ, where: KE is the initial kinetic energy of the projectile θ is the angle of projection Therefore, the answer is true.

questions.text: A 1 kg ball moving in some direction at 8 m/sec hits a 4 kg ball moving at 6 m/sec in the same direction. Assume that the 1 kg ball stops after collision. What will be the velocity, after collision, of the 4 kg ball if the 1 kg ball stops?

The momentum of the system before the collision is: p = mv = (1 kg)(8 m/s) + (4 kg)(6 m/s) = 40 kg m/s where: m is the mass of the ball v is the velocity of the ball The momentum of the system after the collision is: p = mv = (4 kg)(v) where v is the velocity of the 4 kg ball after the collision. Setting these two equations equal to each other, we get: (1 kg)(8 m/s) + (4 kg)(6 m/s) = (4 kg)(v) Solving for v, we get: v = 8 m/s Therefore, the velocity of the 4 kg ball after the collision is 8 m/s in the same direction as the initial velocity of the 1 kg ball.

questions.text: At 10 kg object collides with stationary 5 kg object and after collision they stick together and move forward with velocity 4ms-1What is the velocity with which the 10 kg object hit the second one?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: To determine the velocity with which the 10 kg object hit the second 5 kg object, we can apply the principle of conservation of momentum. According to the principle of conservation of momentum, the total momentum before the collision is equal to the total momentum after the collision.The momentum of an object is calculated as the product of its mass and velocity (p = m * v).Let's denote the initial velocity of the 10 kg object as v1 and the initial velocity of the 5 kg object as v2. Since the second object is stationary, its initial velocity is 0 m/s.The total momentum before the collision is given by:Initial momentum = (mass1 * velocity1) + (mass2 * velocity2) = (10 kg * v1) + (5 kg * 0 m/s) = 10 kg * v1After the collision, the two objects stick together and move forward with a velocity of 4 m/s. Let's denote this final velocity as v_f.The total momentum after the collision is given by:Final momentum = (mass1 + mass2) * v_f = (10 kg + 5 kg) * 4 m/s = 15 kg * 4 m/s = 60 kg m/sAccording to the principle of conservation of momentum, the total momentum before the collision is equal to the total momentum after the collision:10 kg * v1 = 60 kg m/sSolving for v1:v1 = (60 kg m/s) / 10 kgv1 = 6 m/sTherefore, the velocity with which the 10 kg object hit the second 5 kg object is 6 m/s.

questions.text: Time rate of change of momentum is equal to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The time rate of change of momentum is indeed equal to force. This relationship is described by Newton's second law of motion, which states that force is equal to the rate of change of momentum. When a force is applied to an object, it causes a change in its momentum over time.

Velocity represents the rate at which an object changes its position with respect to time, but it is not directly related to the time rate of change of momentum. Velocity is related to momentum but does not represent the rate of change of momentum itself.

This option states that the time rate of change of momentum is equal to impulse. Impulse is a concept in physics that represents the change in momentum of an object when a force acts upon it for a certain period of time. It is defined as the product of force and the time interval over which the force acts. Mathematically, impulse is given by the equation Impulse = Force × Time. While impulse is related to the change in momentum, it is not equal to the time rate of change of momentum.

This option suggests that both option A (Force) and option C (Impulse) are correct and represent the time rate of change of momentum. However, based on the correct answer given in the question, option D is not the correct choice.

questions.explanation: To summarize, the correct option in this question is A) Force. The time rate of change of momentum is indeed equal to force according to Newton's second law of motion.

questions.text: The rate of change of momentum for a freely falling object is always:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The rate of change of momentum for a freely falling object is always equal to its weight. This is because the only force acting on a freely falling object is the force of gravity, which is equal to the object's weight. Newton's second law of motion states that the rate of change of momentum of an object is directly proportional to the force acting on the object, and the direction of the change of momentum is in the same direction as the force. Therefore, the rate of change of momentum for a freely falling object is always equal to its weight, and the direction of the change of momentum is downwards. In mathematical terms, the rate of change of momentum is given by the following equation: F = m * a where F is the force, m is the mass of the object, and a is the acceleration of the object. In the case of a freely falling object, the force is equal to the weight of the object, so we have: F = mg

The rate of change of momentum for a freely falling object is never greater than its weight. This is because the force of gravity is always pulling the object down, and the rate of change of momentum is proportional to the force. Therefore, the rate of change of momentum can never be greater than the force of gravity, which is equal to the object's weight.

The rate of change of momentum can be lesser than the weight if the object is moving upwards. In this case, the force of gravity is still pulling the object down, but the object is also moving upwards due to its initial velocity. The rate of change of momentum is then equal to the difference between the force of gravity and the object's upwards velocity.

The rate of change of momentum can also be zero if the object is neither moving upwards nor downwards. In this case, the force of gravity is balanced by the object's upwards velocity, and the object is in free fall. The rate of change of momentum is then equal to zero.

questions.text: Which is a statement of the principle of conservation of momentum?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: A force is equal to the rate of change of momentum of the body upon which it acts.
options.explanation: This statement is known as Newton's second law of motion, which relates force, mass, and acceleration. It does not directly represent the principle of conservation of momentum.

options.text: In a perfectly elastic collision, the relative momentum of the bodies before impact is equal to their relative momentum after impact.
options.explanation: This statement describes the conservation of relative momentum in a perfectly elastic collision. In an elastic collision, both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved. However, this option specifically mentions relative momentum, which is not a general statement of the principle of conservation of momentum.

options.text: The momentum of a body is the product of the mass of the body and its velocity.
options.explanation: This statement defines momentum, which is indeed the product of mass and velocity. While it provides a key understanding of momentum, it does not encompass the principle of conservation of momentum.

options.text: The total momentum of a system of interacting bodies remains constants, providing no external force acts
options.explanation: This statement represents the principle of conservation of momentum. According to this principle, the total momentum of a system of interacting bodies (in the absence of external forces) remains constant before and after the interaction. It implies that the total momentum of the system is conserved, regardless of the individual changes in momentum for each body involved.

questions.explanation: Let's analyze each option to determine which one represents the principle of conservation of momentum: A) A force is equal to the rate of change of momentum of the body upon which it acts: This statement is known as Newton's second law of motion, which relates force, mass, and acceleration. questions.text: A projectile is launched at 45° to the horizontal with a force of initial kinetic energy "E". Assuming air resistance to be negligible, what will be the kinetic energy of the project when it reaches its highest point?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The following is the solution: When a projectile is launched at an angle of 45 degrees to the horizontal, its horizontal and vertical components of velocity are equal. So, the horizontal velocity (Vx) of the projectile at the highest point is the same as the initial velocity (Vi) multiplied by the cosine of the launch angle: Vx = Vi cos(45 degrees) Since we are considering only the horizontal component of velocity at the highest point, the kinetic energy (K.E-highest) at the highest point can be calculated as follows: K.E-highest = (1/2) x m x Vx2 Substituting the expression for Vx: K.E-highest = (1/2) x m x (Vi cos(45degrees))2 Now, cos(45 degrees) is equal to √2/2: K.E-highest = (1/2) x m x (Vi √2 /2)2 = (1/2) x m x (Vi2 (2 /4)) = (1/2) x m x (Vi2 / 2) Finally, since the initial kinetic energy (Ki) is given as "i," we can write the equation as: K.E-highest = Ki / 2 Therefore, the kinetic energy of the projectile at its highest point will be half of its initial kinetic energy, i.e., K.E-highest = Ki / 2 which can also be written as 0.5 Ki or as the question asked in terms of E, it will become 0.5 E, hence option A is correct.
